text:
The article starts by briefly summarizing the division of Cieszyn Silesia in 1920. The second section describes the structure of the Polish minority in the Zaolzie region and Czech language policy in relation to it. The third, central section discusses linguistic communication in contemporary Cieszin Silesia. Taking into account both written and oral forms and different contexts — formal and informal — the author examines the status and the functions of the Polish language in today’s Czech part of the region of the Olza River and, on the other hand, the Czech language within the current activities connected with the divided city.

Taking into account real language data, the article deals with the topics such as lexical borrowings from non-Slavic languages, morphological adaptation of loanwords, pragmatic function of diminutives and problems of interlinguistic homonymy as well as orthography and typography of letterforms and spellings, placed in their cultural context. In the final part the authors show how bilingualism is visualized in everyday communication in the border cities and how it is shown differently in the Polish and Czech parts of the border, which testifies to the asymmetry of language identification on both sides.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
